coredata.dc:description: There is an urgent requirement for the development of sensitive and quick sensors to monitor chromium (VI) due to its substantial carcinogenic and mutagenic properties. A coexisting system of coumarin 334 and diphenylcarbazide (C334/DPC) was used in this study as a fluorescent chemosensor to detect Cr(VI) ions. Upon the addition of Cr(VI), a purple chelate complex (Cr(III)-diphenylcarbazone) was produced, which resulted from the quantitative reaction between Cr(VI) ions and diphenylcarbazide (DPC), whereas no interaction between Cr(VI) and coumarin 334 took place. More interestingly, the absorption spectra of purple (Cr(III)-diphenylcarbazone) complex (λmax = 540 nm) were overlapped with emission and excitation spectra of coumarin 334 (λex/em = 453/492), resulting in the efficient quenching of coumarin 334 (C334) via the inner filter effect. Furthermore, the semi-quantitative estimation of Cr(VI) ion concentration may be achieved by visually watching the progressive color transformation of the probe from yellow to red after the addition different concentration of Cr(VI). The calibration plot for determination of Cr(VI) by this method is ranging from 0.048 to 268 μM. DFT calculations were conducted to enrich our understanding about the mechanism of action. This approach demonstrates an excellent selectivity and sensitivity for Cr(VI) including a detection limit of 48 nM. The new sensor was successfully applied to water samples (tap, mineral, and waste waters). The accuracy was confirmed by the atomic absorption spectroscopy.

head.ce:title: Uncovering an effecient binary system as a chemosensor for visual and fluorescence detection of chromium (VI) in water samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There is an urgent requirement for the development of sensitive and quick sensors to monitor chromium (VI) due to its substantial carcinogenic and mutagenic properties. A coexisting system of coumarin 334 and diphenylcarbazide (C334/DPC) was used in this study as a fluorescent chemosensor to detect Cr(VI) ions. Upon the addition of Cr(VI), a purple chelate complex (Cr(III)-diphenylcarbazone) was produced, which resulted from the quantitative reaction between Cr(VI) ions and diphenylcarbazide (DPC), whereas no interaction between Cr(VI) and coumarin 334 took place. More interestingly, the absorption spectra of purple (Cr(III)-diphenylcarbazone) complex (λmax = 540 nm) were overlapped with emission and excitation spectra of coumarin 334 (λex/em = 453/492), resulting in the efficient quenching of coumarin 334 (C334) via the inner filter effect. Furthermore, the semi-quantitative estimation of Cr(VI) ion concentration may be achieved by visually watching the progressive color transformation of the probe from yellow to red after the addition different concentration of Cr(VI). The calibration plot for determination of Cr(VI) by this method is ranging from 0.048 to 268 μM. DFT calculations were conducted to enrich our understanding about the mechanism of action. This approach demonstrates an excellent selectivity and sensitivity for Cr(VI) including a detection limit of 48 nM. The new sensor was successfully applied to water samples (tap, mineral, and waste waters). The accuracy was confirmed by the atomic absorption spectroscopy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium, classified as a highly toxic heavy metal, exhibits extensive distribution within aquatic environments. It is primarily generated as a byproduct during diverse industrial operations such as electroplating, leather tanning, wood preservation, mining, printing, metal polishing, and the production of pigments and plastics [1,2]. In the environment, chromium exists in two stable oxidation states, namely Cr(III) and Cr(VI) [3]. Chromium (III) in particular is a vital element in biological processes, serving a significant function in the metabolism of proteins and glucose [4]. Conversely, it should be noted that Cr(VI) exhibits significant toxicity as a result of its potent oxidation characteristics, hence enabling facile diffusion via cellular membranes [5,6]. The ingestion of small quantities of Cr(VI) by individuals can lead to its bioaccumulation inside living organisms, resulting in the onset of diverse ailments including genetic defects, skin ulcers, liver and kidney damage and even death [7]. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA) and the World Health Organization (WHO) have established guidelines stating that the permissible limit for the content of Cr(VI) in drinking water should not exceed 0.1 mg L-1 (equivalent to 1.92 μM) and 0.05 mg L-1 (equivalent to 0.96 μM), respectively [8,9]. Therefore, the development of detection technologies is crucial in order to effectively evaluate the amounts of Cr(VI) in various contexts. To date, a range of analytical techniques have been devised with the purpose of directly quantifying chromium (VI) ions, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [10], ICP atomic emission spectrometry [11], electrochemical [12], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [13], etc. However, these techniques are complicated, expensive, not suitable for on-site analyses, and require sophisticated instrumentation. Furthermore, several methodologies exhibit limitations in their ability to differentiate between Cr(VI) and Cr(III), hence offering just the cumulative quantity of chromium [14,15]. Recently, there has been a significant focus on optical sensors owing to its numerous benefits, including rapid reaction time, affordability, inherent sensitivity, real-time monitoring capabilities, and mobility [16]. Fluorescence chemosensors are widely recognized as a very reliable detection technique among a range of optical sensors. They enable the determination of analytes using either fluorescence enhancement or quenching responses [17,18]. As a result, there has been a notable increase in the scholarly attention devoted to the development of luminescent probes towards the specific application of chromium(VI) detection.

Currently, a diverse range of fluorescent reagents have been reported in the literature for the purpose of detecting heavy metals, with a particular emphasis on chromium (VI) ions [5,19–21]. In order to be suitable for implementation in sensor devices, it is imperative that these probes satisfy several key criteria. Initially, they must exhibit a significant alteration in fluorescence intensity, either through quenching or enhancement. Additionally, their excitation maximum should exceed 420 nm, enabling the utilization of cost-effective and reliable light-emitting diodes (LEDs) as a light source. Furthermore, these probes should possess high fluorescence quantum yields, a substantial Stokes' shift, a notable extinction coefficient, and exceptional photostability [22]. In fact, there has been a significant focus on naked-eye-based detection methods recently, primarily because of their straightforward visual identification capabilities, which eliminate the requirement for complex instruments [23]. The integration of both components in the analytical process enhances the method, rendering it more substantial and particularly sought-after in the context of on-site detection.

Various optical sensing strategies, including fluorescence, colorimetry, and chemiluminescence, have been explored to detect Cr(VI) ions in different matrices. Fluorescence sensors have been widely studied for Cr(VI) detection. For instance, the work by Talanta [24] demonstrated the use of coumarin derivatives for the fluorescence detection of Cr(VI). These sensors rely on the quenching or enhancement of fluorescence signals upon interaction with Cr(VI) ions. More recent studies have focused on synthesizing coumarin derivatives with improved sensitivity and selectivity [25]. These sensors exhibit high sensitivity with detection limits in the micromolar range, making them suitable for various applications. Colorimetric detection offers a simple and visual method for detecting Cr(VI) ions. Many studies have developed colorimetric sensors using organic reagents that change color in the presence of Cr(VI). These methods are advantageous due to their simplicity and the ability to perform on-site testing without sophisticated instrumentation. Chemiluminescence-based sensors provide high sensitivity and are used for detecting trace amounts of Cr(VI). These sensors generate light through chemical reactions, which is then measured to determine the presence of Cr(VI). Despite the advancements in optical sensors, there remains a need for methods that combine high sensitivity, selectivity, rapid response, and practical applicability in complex matrices. Our study addresses this gap by introducing a novel binary chemosensor system that combines Coumarin 334 (C334) and Diphenylcarbazide (DPC). Unlike previous studies that utilize single-component systems, our work leverages a binary system combining C334 and DPC. This system exploits the specific interaction between DPC and Cr(VI) to form a Cr-DPC complex, which absorbs the fluorescence emitted by C334 through the inner filter effect (IFE), resulting in enhanced sensitivity and selectivity.

Coumarin 334 has been identified as a suitable candidate for meeting some of the required criteria for the implementation in sensor devices due to its commercial availability, reduced biological toxicity, and lack of necessity for chemical alteration [26]. It demonstrates absorption at a wavelength of 453 nm and exhibits its highest emission intensity at 492 nm. Consequently, it may be effectively stimulated using longwave-emitting LEDs, which minimally induce background light in actual samples. Coumarin 334 has a high quantum yield about 0.89 and displays remarkable photostability, making it a favorable choice to be used in a quenchiometric test [27,28].

Our approach does not require the synthesis of new coumarin derivatives. Instead, we use commercially available C334 and DPC, simplifying the preparation and making the method more accessible for practical applications. The Asian Journal study focuses on visual detection, whereas our method employs fluorescence quenching through IFE. This provides a dual detection capability (both visual and fluorescence), offering greater flexibility and sensitivity in detecting Cr(VI) ions. Where, the binding of Cr (VI) ions to diphenyl carbazide (DPC) under mineral acid conditions results in the formation of a purple complex known as Cr(III)-diphenyl carbazone [29,30]. This purple complex effectively quenches the strong fluorescence of the fluorophore (coumarin 334) due to the inter filter effect (IFE).

Our chemosensor system demonstrates significantly lower detection limits for Cr(VI) ions. Specifically, our method achieves detection limits in the nanomolar range, which is an improvement over the micromolar detection limits reported in Talanta. The combination of C334 and DPC in a binary chemosensor system is novel and has not been previously explored. This system's ability to detect Cr(VI) ions through the formation of a Cr-DPC complex and subsequent fluorescence quenching represents a significant advancement in the field. Our study validates the chemosensor's performance in real water samples, including tap water and river water and in biological sample (mice liver), showcasing its practical utility and robustness in complex matrices. The Talanta study primarily focuses on controlled laboratory conditions without extensive testing in real-world samples. This real-world applicability is crucial for environmental monitoring and public health. The response time of our chemosensor system is significantly shorter, enabling real-time monitoring of Cr(VI) levels. Our system shows high selectivity for Cr(VI) even in the presence of other metal ions, reducing false positives and increasing reliability. In summary, while significant progress has been made in the development of optical sensors for Cr(VI) detection, our study presents a novel approach that combines the advantages of existing methods while addressing their limitations. We believe our work offers a significant advancement in the field and provides a robust, accessible, and highly effective method for detecting Cr(VI) ions.

Coumarin 334 (C334) and diphenyl carbazide (DPC) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (https://www.sigmaaldrich.com). Their chemical structures are shown in scheme1 . All inorganic salts, organic solvents, and mineral acids were of analytical quality and obtained likewise from Sigma-Aldrich.

The ultraviolet–visible spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu UV-1800 UV/Visible Spectrophotometer (https://www.shimadzu.com), employing a quartz cell with a path length of 1.0 cm. The luminescence spectra were measured using a Jasco 6300 spectrofluorometer (https://jascoinc.com), employing a quartz cell with a path length of 1.0 cm. Excitation was achieved using a xenon lamp (150 W). The emission and excitation band widths exhibited a width of 5 nm. The concentration of chromium ions (VI) in the water samples was determined using a PerkinElmer Model 2380 Atomic Absorption Spectrophotometer equipped with a hollow cathode lamp specifically designed for chromium analysis.

2.0 x 10-3 M Stock solutions of metal ions (Cr(VI), Cr(III), Fe(II), Ba(II), Cu(II), Fe(III), Al(III), Mn(II), Zn(II), Ni(II), Mn(VII), Pb(II), Na+, Ca(II), Co(II), Hg(II), and Mg(II)) were prepared. These solutions were obtained by dissolving the respective nitrate or chloride salts in deionized water. A stock solution of diphenyl carbazide with a concentration of 2.0 x 10-2 M was made by dissolving 0.242 g of diphenyl carbazide in 50.0 mL of acetone, which was supplemented with 1 mL of concentrated H2SO4. The stock solution of coumarin 334 was made by dissolving 2.83 mg of the compound in 10.0 mL of ethanol, resulting in a concentration of 1.0 x 10-3 M. The working solutions were stable for two weeks.

The working solutions of Cr(VI) were prepared by transferring various volumes (0, 0.20, 0.50, 0.90, 2.8, 5.0, 9.5, 28.5, 50, 104, 192, 288, 384.5, 480.5, 718, 865, and 1340 µL) of 2.0 x 10-3 M Cr(VI) stock solution into 10.00 mL volumetric flasks which contain 200 µL of acidified diphenyl carbazide stock solution and 100 µl of coumarin 334 stock solutions then completed to volume with ethanol. Herewith, Cr(VI) concentrations of (0, 0.048, 0.096, 0.192, 0.59, 0.96, 1.9, 5.7, 10.0, 20.8, 38.4, 57.6, 76.9, 96.1, 143.6, 173.0, and 268 μM), respectively, were obtained.

The fluorescence measurements were recorded at an excitation wavelength = 453 nm and an emission wavelength = 492 nm. The study was conducted by measuring the quenching of luminescence at a wavelength of 492 nm for the probe in the presence and absence of different concentrations of Cr(VI). An interference analysis was conducted to investigate the effects of several foreign ions on a solution containing Cr(VI) ions at a concentration of 10.0 μM. The concentrations of the interfering ions were varied, and the experiment was carried out under controlled circumstances. The spectroscopic measurements were conducted under room temperature.

The tap water samples were obtained from laboratory faucets after a duration of 10 min of uninterrupted water flow. Mineral water samples were procured from a nearby marketplace, namely from the Baraka company. The samples were evaluated in their unpurified state. Wastewater samples were obtained at a collecting site for industrial effluent located within an industrial zone including chemical and concrete firms in New Damietta, Egypt. The wastewater samples underwent filtration using cellulose membrane filters with a pore size of 0.45 μm. Following filtration, the water samples were enriched with a standard solution containing Cr(VI) ions and then subjected to analysis using the recommended probe. The concentration of Cr(VI) found in real samples was compared and confirmed by the atomic absorption spectroscopy.

To obtain the most stable geometries for the studied fluorophore (C334) and Cr(III)-diphenylcarbazone complex, Density Functional Theory (DFT) implemented in Gaussian09 software [31] was utilized. In particular, B3LYP [32–34] functional that was successfully to treat various systems [35–38] in combination with 6–311 + G(d,p) basis set was used to treat all the atoms except Cr(III) which was represented by LANL2DZ basis set on the basis of effective core potential (ECP). To confirm the identification of the obtained stable geometries as stationary points, frequency calculations at the optimization level of theory were conducted on the optimized chemical compounds and no imaginary frequency was emerged. to obtain the frontier molecular orbitals for the studied molecules and explain their chemical reactivity, Gaussian09 utilities were also used. The determined frontier molecular orbitals, highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) were visualized using Gaussian view software.

Mice liver samples, containing pre-determined concentration of chromium Cr(VI), were kindly provided by biochemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Damietta University. A 1.3 g sample of liver tissue was digested in a minimal volume of concentrated HNO3 and heated to ensure complete digestion. The solid residue was then dissolved in deionized water, then detected the same way as real water samples. The concentration of Cr(VI) found in real samples was compared and confirmed by the atomic absorption spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The C334/DPC system as a sensor for Cr(VI) detection
ce:section.ce:para: Several fluorescent reagents have been mentioned in the literature for the purpose of detecting heavy metals, such as Cr(VI) ions [39]. In order to be suitable for implementation in sensor technology, these probes should meet various features including a substantial alteration in fluorescence intensity, either through enhancement or quenching. In addition to an excitation maximum exceeding 420 nm, which enables the utilization of cost-effective and reliable light-emitting diodes (LEDs) as a light source. These effective probes should also be characterized with high quantum yields; a significant Stokes' shift, and exceptional resistance to photodegradation. Therefore, Coumarin 334 (C334) was selected to satisfy several of these aforementioned criteria in addition to its commercial availability and lack of necessity for chemical modification. The substance exhibits absorption at a wavelength of 453 nm and emits light most intensely at 492 nm. Consequently, it may be stimulated by longwave-emitting LEDs, which produce little background luminescence in actual samples. In fact, C334 has a quantum yield over 0.89 and demonstrates remarkable photostability, rendering its advantageous for use in a quenchiometric assessment. However, it does not provide any sites that can bond with Cr(VI) complexes. Consequently, two compounds are employed for the purpose of detecting Cr(VI). The first one is diphenyl carbazide (DPC), which is a widely recognized non-fluorescent and chromogenic chelator for Cr(VI)in addition to C334, that exhibits fluorescence properties. Therefore, the interaction between Cr(VI) and DPC resulted in the formation of a highly purple conjugate with a maximum absorption wavelength of 547 nm. The conjugate then absorbs the fluorescence emission of C334, released at a wavelength of 492 nm leading to a reduction in the intensity of fluorescence due possibly to an inner-filter effect.

The optical properties of C334/DPC system as a prob were studied by fluorescence emission spectra and UV − vis absorption spectra in ethanol. The system showed a maximum absorption wavelength at 454 nm due to the n–π* transitions of C334 while there is no absorption band for DPC (Fig. 1 a) when it treated with different cations such as Cr(III), Fe(II), Ba(II), Cu(II), Fe(III), Al(III), Mn(II), Zn(II), Ni(II), Mn(VII), Pb(II), Na(I), Ca(II), Co(II), Hg(II), and Mg(II). Interestingly, the system showed a new maximum absorption wavelength at 547 nm only when treated with Cr(VI) ions resulting in the formation of a purple conjugate indicating the remarkable selectivity of the (C334/DPC) prob toward Cr(VI) ions (Fig. 1 a & 1b). To further evaluate this selectivity feature, a similar approach was followed at the ions were introduced into a solution containing the C334/DPC sensor and an emission band at 492 nm using an excitation wavelength = 452 nm was introduced, Fig. 1 c and 1d. Notably, the fluorescence emission of the (C334/DPC) was significantly attenuated only after the introduction of Cr(VI) while remained unaffected by the presence of other metal ions at 492 nm. In our study, C334 and DPC are used as separate entities in the chemosensor system. They do not react with each other directly. Instead, DPC interacts with Cr(VI) to form a complex, which then absorbs the fluorescence emitted by C334 through the inner filter effect (IFE). This absorption results in the observed fluorescence quenching. The system leverages the specific interaction of DPC with Cr(VI) and the subsequent quenching of C334 fluorescence to achieve the detection of Cr(VI) ions. This approach is effective for creating a selective and sensitive detection system without requiring a direct interaction between C334 and DPC. Similar system has been employed in our pervious study where a separate reagent reacts with the analyte, and the resulting complex influences the fluorescence of another component through IFE [22].

The C334/DPC is a coexisting system wherein diphenyl carbazide (DPC) serves as an indicator for the presence of Cr (VI) ions under acidic conditions. This system operates on the principle of oxidizing diphenyl carbazide (DPC) through chromium (VI), resulting in the formation of diphenyl carbazone (DPCA), while concurrently reducing Cr (VI) ions to Cr (III) ions, Scheme 2
. Subsequently, the Cr (III) ion undergoes complexation with DPCA, resulting in the formation of a conjugate exhibiting a purple coloration (Fig. 1b). This conjugate exhibits an absorption band throughout the visible range of 400–600 nm, with a maximum absorption at λmax = 547 nm (Fig. 1a). The reactions can possibly be described as in the scheme below while the octahedral geometry of Cr(III) has been fulfilled with the coordination of two ethanol groups:
This mechanism is supported by literature that describes the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) in the presence of diphenylcarbazide and the subsequent formation of a Cr(III)-DPC complex [9,40].

The conjugate subsequently undergoes absorption of the fluorescence emission of coumarin 334, which is released at a wavelength of 492 nm. This absorption leads to a reduction in the fluorescence intensity of coumarin 334 at 492 nm due to the occurrence of an inner-filter effect. UV–visible and fluorescence analysis were performed to confirm this speculation. According to the data presented in Fig. 2 a, it is evident that the free C334 exhibited a significant absorption peak at a wavelength of 453 nm in ethanol. This absorption peak may be ascribed to the π-π* transition of the conjugate plane of coumarin 334. When comparing the absorption properties of DPC alone, which does not exhibit any absorption bands in the visible spectral range, it can be observed that the addition of DPC does not result in the appearance of any additional absorption band. This observation demonstrates the absence of interaction occurring at electronic energy levels. In contrast, the presence of Cr(VI) has resulted in the emergence of notable absorption bands within the visible spectrum at 547 nm, as seen in Fig. 2 b & 2c. The absorption bands exhibit significant overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of C334, as seen in Fig. 2 d. The observed decreasing in fluorescence emission of C334 in the existence of chromium (VI) can be attributed to the operation of IFE-based quenching, which arises due to spectrum overlap. In the present experimental investigation, the Cr(VI) solution was introduced into the coumarin 334 solution in the absence of DPC. It is acknowledged that Coumarin 334 contains acetyl and keto groups that could potentially bind metal ions. However, our current UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra data do not show significant changes indicating a strong interaction or complex formation with Cr(VI) ions. Specifically, the UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 2e) of 10 μM Coumarin 334 show minimal changes upon the addition of 96.1 μM Cr(VI), and the fluorescence spectra do not exhibit significant quenching or a shift in emission wavelength (Fig. 2f).

These observations suggest that under the acidic conditions used in our experiments, the acetyl and keto groups in Coumarin 334 do not significantly interact with Cr(VI) ions. Possible reasons for this include the specific acidic conditions, which may influence the ionization state of the functional groups, and the concentrations of Cr(VI) and Coumarin 334 used, which might not be optimal for complex formation. Our findings are consistent with other studies where specific conditions or ion pairs did not result in significant interactions.

The optimization of detection conditions is a critical aspect to ensure the reliability and accuracy of the C334-DPC chemosensor for Cr(VI) detection. In our study, we optimized several parameters, including the concentration of diphenylcarbazide (DPC), the concentration of Coumarin 334 (C334), and the response time. This section provides a comprehensive overview of the optimization process, supported by data graphs for intuitive observation.

The concentration of DPC plays a significant role in the formation of the Cr-DPC complex, which is essential for the fluorescence quenching mechanism. We evaluated the effect of different DPC concentrations on the sensitivity of the calibration curve for Cr(VI) detection. The results are summarized in Fig. 1S and table 1 S. The data indicate that increasing the concentration of DPC improves the linearity and sensitivity of the calibration curve, with 400 μM DPC providing the best results (R2 = 0.985). Therefore, 400 μM was selected as the optimal concentration for subsequent experiments.

The concentration of C334 is crucial for achieving effective fluorescence quenching in the presence of the Cr-DPC complex. We examined the quenching efficiency at various C334 concentrations while maintaining a constant DPC concentration of 400 μM and a Cr(VI) concentration of 9.6 μM. The results demonstrate that higher concentrations of C334 lead to more significant fluorescence quenching. The optimal concentration of C334 was determined to be 10 µM, as it provided the best balance between quenching efficiency and signal intensity.

The response time is a vital parameter to ensure the rapid and reliable detection of Cr(VI). We conducted measurements of fluorescence intensity at regular intervals to determine the optimal response time for the C334-DPC chemosensor at a wavelength of 492 nm at regular intervals of 5.0 min throughout the course of one hour. The obtained data are illustrated in Fig. 3 . The results indicate that the majority of the fluorescence quenching occurs within the first 5 min, with the signal stabilizing thereafter. Therefore, a response time of 5 min was selected for all subsequent measurements to ensure rapid and accurate detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of the probe to Cr(VI) (Calibration plots and visual detection)
ce:section.ce:para: The emission and excitation spectra of a solution containing 10.0 μM of C334 and 400.0 μM of 1,5-diphenylcarbazide with the addition of various concentrations of Cr(VI) (see the methods section) 0, 0.048, 0.096, 0.192, 0.59, 0.96, 1.9, 5.7, 10.0, 20.8, 38.4, 57.6, 76.9, 96.1, 143.6, 173.0, and 268 μM) in ethanol were monitored and analyzed at room temperature as shown in Fig. 4 a. Notably, the fluorescence of C334 is effectively quenched by the presence of chromium (VI) concentrations ranging from 0.048 to 268 μM. The fluorescence intensity of C334 at a wavelength of 492 nm exhibited a progressive decline as the Cr(VI) concentration increased in solution, eventually reaching a plateau. The addition of Cr(VI) leads to the formation of the Cr-DPC complex, which has distinct absorption characteristics compared to the individual components. This complex formation can cause changes in the local environment around the Coumarin 334 (C334) molecules, leading to a slight shift in the fluorescence emission peak. Also, the IFE mechanism involves the absorption of the excitation or emission light by the absorbing species (Cr-DPC complex) in the solution. This absorption can alter the effective excitation wavelength and the emission profile of the fluorophore (C334). The overlap of the absorption spectrum of the Cr-DPC complex with the excitation and emission spectra of C334 leads to the observed quenching and the shift in the emission peak.

The calibration curve of our probe for measuring Cr(VI) concentrations is represented by a relationship between the luminescence intensity (y-axis) and the logarithm of Cr(VI) concentrations (x-axis) under ideal conditions (see Fig. 4 c). The observed effect can be explained as the concentration of 0.048 µM of Cr(VI), resulted in a reduction of 3.3 % in the initial fluorescence intensity. At a concentration of 268.0 μM of Cr(VI), the fluorescence exhibits a reduction to 87.77 % of its initial value. Cr(VI) was evaluated within the range of 0.0–268 μM with three replicates in ethanol.

The calibration plot has a sigmoidal shape which reveals a dynamic range from 0.0 to 268.0 μM (Fig. 4 c and Table 1 ) as obtained by a logistic sigmoidal fit (R2 = 0.998). The calibration curve exhibits a linear range ranging from 20.8 to 268.0 μM. It can be described by

(R2 = 0.998) (Fig. 5 ). In order to fully use the dynamic range, a logistic sigmoidal regression model was utilized, with a range of 0.048 to 268 μM (R2 = 0.998, n = 3). The limit of detection is 48 nM. this is lower by a factor of 10 compared to earlier fluorescence sensor [41]. The detection limit (LOD) was calculated based on the standard deviation of the response (σ) and the slope of the calibration curve (S) using the formula: LOD = 3 σ / S where, σ is the standard deviation of the response and S is the slope of the calibration curve.

To more effectively evaluate the efficiency of our probe compared to established ones, we have compiled a summary of previously employed chemosensors for measuring Cr(VI) in Table 2
[42–58]. Interestingly, this later comparison revealed the higher sensitivity and better streamlined operational process of the proposed probe in this study. Furthermore, the limit of detection of our approach is better suited for the analysis and monitoring of Cr(VI) concentrations in ambient samples of water. The method we propose offers an expanded linear range and consequently, the majority of samples will not need any further dilution. The inner filter effect approach mentioned in this study demonstrates more sensitivity when compared to the prior colorimetric method utilizing diphenylcarbazide for the detection of Cr(VI) [55,59]. Visually, a strong color gradient was obtained with increasing the Cr(VI) concentrations (Fig. 4 d). The solutions have a yellow color in the absence of Cr(VI). The solutions show first traces of faint pink color at 0.95 μM, and with increasing the concentration of Cr(VI), their color becomes deep pink and red. According to the WHO (World Health Organization) and the European Water Quality Directive [60], the recommended Chromium concentration in drinking water is to be below 0.96 µM (0.05 mg L−1). This corresponds nicely to concentrations that induce the pink color that can be seen by eye-vision. Also, the yellow-green fluorescence emission of coumarin 334 exhibited a progressive decrease until it was fully extinguished when exposed to ultraviolet light at a wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. 4 (b)). Hence, a visual control of the threshold level of Cr(VI) concentrations in water samples is feasible with this method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of the detection system
ce:section.ce:para: Probes should have the capability toto distinguish the analyte of interest from any other species that may potentially act as competitors. Therefore, the selectivity test conducted here involved measuring the fluorescence change of the probe (C334/DPC) after treating it with 40 µM Cr(VI) ions in the presence of 100 µM of various interfering metals in ethanol. Subsequently, the impact of Cr(VI) and the combined presence of Cr(VI) and Mn+ on the fluorescence of the C334/DPC was assessed (Fig. 6 ). The findings indicate that the presence of other ions had minimal impact on the fluorescence quenching of Cr(VI) to C334/DPC, and thereby their influence may be disregarded. The observation implythat the C334/DPC sensor exhibited selectivity towards Cr(VI) while displaying insensitivity towards other metal ions present in the mixes.

The IFE mechanism involves the absorption of the excitation or emission light by the absorbing species (Cr-DPC complex) in the solution. This absorption can alter the effective excitation wavelength and the emission profile of the fluorophore (C334). The overlap of the absorption spectrum of the Cr-DPC complex with the excitation and emission spectra of C334 leads to the observed quenching and the shift in the emission peak. The UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. 2a) show that the Cr-DPC complex exhibits a significant absorption peak at 540 nm, which overlaps with the excitation and emission spectra of C334. This overlap is a strong indication of the IFE mechanism, where the Cr-DPC complex absorbs the emitted light from C334, leading to fluorescence quenching.

The fluorescence spectra (Fig. 4a) demonstrate the quenching effect upon the addition of various concentrations of Cr(VI). The progressive decline in fluorescence intensity with increasing Cr(VI) concentration supports the IFE mechanism, where the Cr-DPC complex acts as an inner filter, reducing the fluorescence signal of C334.

To further confirm the role of IFE in the fluorescence of C334 suppression, the observed fluorescence intensity was corrected by utilizing the equation.
(1)

) / 2
This equation provides a simple correction method and has been utilized previously [61,62]. FObs and Fcorr are the fluorescence intensity of fluorophore before and after the IFE correction, respectively. Aex and Aem are the UV–vis absorption value of the co-existing system (C334/DPC) having analytes Cr(VI) at fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths, respectively. They are listed in Table S 2. The simplified Eq. (1) is applicable for correcting the fluorescence IFE in a spectrum obtained using a fluorescence cuvette of 1 cm × 1 cm dimensions. The suppressed efficiency (%E) was calculated for both observed and corrected fluorescence according to equation (2) [18].
(2)
E % = ( 1 - F / F 0 ) × 100
Plots of %E of the corrected and observed fluorescence of C334/DPC system versus Cr(V) concentrations (Fig. S3) concluded that IFE is a major contributor in the fluorescence quenching by about 78.2 % of the overall suppressed efficiency for Cr(VI).

DFT calculation was carried out in the follow-up experiments to further understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Cr(III)-diphenylcarbazone complex toward the C334 fluorophore. C334 was investigated in acidic medium (which was essential for the preparation of Cr(III)-diphenylcarbazone complex) and therefore it will mainly exist in its protonated form. We initiated our calculations by exploring the possible protonation states for the fluorophore and our calculations supported the thermodynamic preference of present isomer, in which the keton group of the ring is protonated, with an energy difference of −0.28 ev. The UV/vis absorption spectrum of Cr(III)-diphenylcarbazone in acidic condition shows a characteristic absorption peak at 540 nm, which well overlaps with the excitation and emission bands of C334. This suggests that fluorescence quenching may be related to FRET, IFE, or electron transfer mechanisms [1]. The photoinduced electron-transfer (PET) process between C334 and Cr(III)-diphenylcarbazone was investigated based on DFT calculations as shown in Fig. 7 below.

The molecular geometries for C334 and Cr(III)-diphenylcarbazone were fully characterized using the DFT functional mentioned in the methods section. For Cr(III)-diphenylcarbazone in particular, the proposed octahedral geometry in Scheme 2 has been successfully characterized. Each ofThe relative energies of the HOMO (EHOMO) of C334 is found to be −0.3429 ev that is higher than (ELUMO) of Cr(III)-diphenylcarbazone (−0.4213), Fig. 7, preventing the transfer of the excited electrons from C334 fluorophore to Cr(III)-diphenylcarbazone. This observation clearly confirms unfeasibility of the PET mechanism from an energetic perspective. On the other hand, C334 is most likely to exist in its protonated form and thus, an electrostatic repulsion between C334 and Cr(III) cation in the-Cr-diphenylcarbazone complex is expected to occur. Therefore, the distance between C334 and Cr(III)-diphenylcarbazone complex is expected to be greater than 10 nm, implying the less possibility of the FRET mechanism [63]. Therefore, we suggest that the IFE effect of Cr(III)-diphenylcarbazone complex on the fluorescence quenching of C334 for the detection of Cr(VI) is mostly preferred to take place than PET and FRET.

To evaluate the practical applicability of the C334-DPC chemosensor, we conducted experiments to detect Cr(VI) in real-world samples, including tap water, mineral water, wastewater, and biological samples such as mice liver. The detailed procedure for detecting Cr(VI) in various water samples (tap, mineral, and wastewater) is described in Section 2.5. The results are summarized in Table 3 , indicating the recovery rates and relative standard deviations for the spiked Cr(VI) concentrations. Mice liver samples containing predetermined concentrations of Cr(VI) were kindly provided by the Biochemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Damietta University. To prepare the liver samples for analysis, a 1.3 g sample of liver tissue was digested in a minimal volume of concentrated HNO3 and heated to ensure complete digestion. The solid residue was then dissolved in deionized water. The detection procedure was carried out similarly to the water samples, using the C334-DPC chemosensor. The fluorescence measurements were taken to determine the Cr(VI) concentration in the digested liver samples. The concentration of Cr(VI) detected in the liver samples was compared and confirmed by atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS).

The results of the detection of Cr(VI) in mice liver samples are presented in Table 4 . The recovery rates for spiked Cr(VI) concentrations in liver samples ranged from 99.6 % to 100.6 %, with relative standard deviation percentages (RSD%) ranging from 0.071 % to 0.310 % (n = 3). These results demonstrate the high accuracy and reliability of the C334-DPC chemosensor in detecting Cr(VI) in biological samples.

Our results are consistent with and, in many cases, surpass the performance of other reported methods for Cr(VI) detection in biological samples. For example: The use of diphenylcarbazide (DPC) for colorimetric detection has been widely reported, but our method integrates the fluorescence quenching mechanism, which enhances sensitivity and selectivity [55]. Similar studies utilizing nanomaterials such as gold nanoparticles for Cr(VI) detection have shown detection limits in the micromolar range, whereas our method achieves detection limits in the nanomolar range, providing higher sensitivity[8]. In conclusion, our method demonstrates superior performance in terms of sensitivity and practical applicability for detecting Cr(VI) in biological samples, making it a valuable tool for environmental and health monitoring.

In this study, we have effectively designed a novel fluorescent probe that is both simple and sensitive, offering accurate and reliable detection of Cr(VI) in water samples. This probe is based on the IFE (Inner Filter Effect) of C334, which serves as a fluorescence probe with diphenylcarbazide, acting as a recognition element for Cr(VI). Under optimal experimental conditions, the Cr(III)-diphenylcarbazone complex acts as a fluorescence quencher for the C334. The current concept based on IFE enables the development of a fluorescent assay in a more simplified, efficient, and cost-effective manner, as it eliminates the need for any modification or labeling procedures of C334 and DPC. We have also investigated the impacts of particular experimental conditions and elucidate the likely underlying quenching mechanism utilizing a combined experimental and DFT approaches. The suggested methodology has a notable level of selectivity when compared to other metal ions, particularly Cr(III). The experimental findings demonstrate a wide range of detection capabilities, spanning from 0.048 to 268 μM, with a strong correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.998, n = 3). Furthermore, the detection limit for Cr(VI) is quite low, measuring at 48 nM. More importantly, this method has the potential to be used in practical application to monitor Cr(VI) in real environmental samples without interference. In fact, the detection of Cr(VI) using our proposed probe was visible with the naked eye affirming it as a cost-effective approach for sensing applications.
